data.title: Cenovus Energy: Weak Quarter Is The Time To Buy
data.description:
Cenovus Energy (NYSE:CVE) reported a weak quarter. Left out of the conversation was a line in the report and conference call by management that the sale of some production was delayed. Such a comment is usually a sign by management that if they wait, they will get better prices. There was also the redemption of some preferred stock which decreases financial leverage for common stock investors. However, there was a concern about refining and another concern that debt climbed in a quarter that had negative free cash flow.

But quarterly variations in this very volatile industry are not all that uncommon. Furthermore, I have long held that the refineries were so badly managed by Husky that it would take some time to fix the situation. Management mentioned on the conference call what all was done in the latest fiscal year. But the market probably will not be satisfied until profits are where the market thinks they should be. Management, to their credit, indicated that there was more work to be done.

Refining

On the other hand, both the Toledo and Superior refineries ran all year while both improved operations. Superior was basically a major construction project that really needs a couple of years to run "and get the bugs out" that any major project has. A past article covered the Toledo fire and subsequent acquisition of the BP (BP) interest.

(Note: Cenovus Energy is a Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

The problem with something like this is that it gets solved first, and the benefits show up next. Notice that restart costs for both Toledo and Superior were mentioned. Restarting anything large is usually a messy process that costs serious money. That whole process has now ended. Next comes optimization for both of them.

The overall process is on the slow side because there are a lot of fixed assets. That generally means one thing gets fixed and management reviews the results before deciding on the next project.

Making things even harder for investors is the fluctuating spreads that are normal for refining. If the conference call was any indication, patience in the investment community is wearing thin. Management deserves a lot of credit for doing things right and resisting that "wearing thin".

Similar Situation

This problem is similar to the situation with Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) in that the CEO finally did a road presentation and just stated "it took two years" which was about two years longer than the market had patience for. The stock price paid the price. But now, finally, management is at the point where things are fixed, and the original strategy can be executed.

The big deal is that for all the talk about doing things right and supposedly rewarding that "right". The fact is that Mr. Market is very impatient. Doing things right is ok with the market as long as it is done in about two minutes. Now long-term, there is no doubt that the market rewards superior strategies. But the short-term pricing action can be a royal headache for management.

Growth

Another thing a weak quarter does is take the market concentration away from the long-term profitable growth story. Mr. Market often assumes that a weak performance will continue "forever" despite the progress made here to make sure that is not the case long-term.

Management emphasized the continuing growth projects so that the growth story shown above will continue. Now this company has long augmented organic growth with opportunistic acquisitions. That means the slow steady growth of a typically large company like this one will have an occasional growth spurt from an acquisition.

Similarly, both cash flow and free funds flow have grown even if the quarterly and annual results are on the erratic side due to commodity price volatility. Well run thermal producers like this one tend to cash flow on the generous side over time.

Earnings Summary

(Note: Cenovus Energy is a Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Even though this is widely regarded as a weak quarter, cash flow was still on the generous side.

As shown above, this is a company with a growth capital budget as well as paying a dividend, repurchasing some preferred stock and (from time to time) buying back common stock as well. While debt did climb a little bit, the net debt figure is well within a reasonable range.

The stock is also likely punished because capital expenditures climbed at a time of weak pricing. Business schools teach that the safest time to expand is when it can be profitably done during a cyclical downturn or a time of industry weakness. Meanwhile, Mr. Market is looking for free cash flow and dividends "yesterday". He will likely be happy when the capital projects provide both the dividends and the free cash flow. He is not happy now.

Management can easily get the net debt figure to C$4 billion (which is the original target) again without a whole lot of stress on the cash flow. However, it would be hard to tell that from some of the conference call questions and other comments. For that reason, it may be best to get that net debt figure back to where it belongs to stop all the market worrying about it.

The biggest lesson for investors is that quarterly variations are the norm in this business. So, it is probably best to take advantage of the market attitude when a quarter like this gets reported.

Traders often make a good living by selling the good quarters and waiting for an inevitable quarter like this. Some have options strategies to enhance the income made from the stock price volatility.

But for long-term investors, the growth story remains intact even if the quarterly swings make for a bumpy ride.

Summary

Despite the record production and several solid growth projects on the table, the market focused upon the weak current quarterly report. But this industry has those reports interspaced with strong quarterly reports. The key is that there is nothing wrong with the company.

Finances are in good shape here which seriously reduces the main risk in this industry. More importantly, thermal operations often cash flow more than adequately even when profits are poor because a thermal project often has a large upfront cash requirement.

This management made a lot of acquisitions and shed a lot of partners in the process of gaining full control of its operations. Now it is going to take some time to straighten things out. But work is under way to do just that even if the gradual improvement shown is a bit slow for the market's patience.

The recent stock price weakness strengthens the strong buy case. Mr. Market will come around as the benefits of the growth projects and the margin improvement projects begin to show.

In the meantime, the base dividend was substantially increased in the current fiscal year. Management predicts more base dividend increases as all the profitable projects show results.

I would tend to hang onto this stock until the growth and profit improvement story changes. That is likely to be well into the future.

Risks

The biggest risk here is the loss of market patience with refining results because the pace of improving the situation is not to the liking of the market. This results in stock price action that can really give an investor a stomachache. It should work out in the long-run. But getting there is going to be bumpy.

Any upstream operation is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn could materially change the future outlook of the company.

To a certain extent the refining operations will protect against a widening of the discount to light oil benchmarks because the products made from refining are value added products that sell for the same amount regardless of the raw materials. This is probably the largest single benefit to the company at the current time.

Finances are investment grade which minimizing financial risks that smaller players face.

Management here has an above average track record in the long run. A loss of key personnel could prove to be a challenge to replace because of that record.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ariel Investments Small Cap Value Concentrated Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Markets worldwide defied expectations in 2024 led by the dominating performance of mega-cap technology stocks known as the “magnificent seven.1” Even though gains have been concentrated, the results of the U.S. election, strong earnings momentum and a robust labor market drove bullish sentiment. This ebullience extended beyond the equity market: gold delivered its best year since 2010, bitcoin more than doubled and the U.S. dollar neared a 20-year high. Meanwhile, uncertainty surrounding the new U.S. administration’s policies and the Federal Reserve signaling slower than anticipated easing in the year ahead drove small cap issues sharply lower in December. Although uncertainty and volatility are likely to remain elevated, the patient investor knows “stock prices trade on fundamentals. And when those solid fundamentals shine through, share prices rise. 2” Against this backdrop, the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite declined -0.54% gross of fees (-0.67% net of fees) in the quarter, outpacing the Russell 2000 Value Index’s -1.06% return, but shy of the +0.33% gain posted by the Russell 2000 Index. Over the trailing one-year period, the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite increased +12.07% (+11.46% net of fees), outperforming the Russell 2000 Value Index’s +8.05% gain and the +11.54% return earned by the Russell 2000 Index.

Several stocks in the portfolio had strong returns in the quarter. Provider of wellness services onboard cruise ships and destination resorts, OneSpaWorld Holdings Ltd. (OSW), traded higher as strong consumer trends drove another significant earnings beat and subsequent raise in full year guidance. Meanwhile, management strengthened the balance sheet by paying down and restructuring its expensive first lien term loan as well as ramped up capital returns to shareholders through buybacks and dividends.

Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd. (NCLH) also advanced over the period following a top- and bottom-line earnings beat and subsequent raise in full-year guidance.

Stronger than anticipated consumer demand, healthy onboard spending, robust pricing, solid cost containment and continued progress on leverage reduction boosted results. Looking ahead, NCLH remains focused on right sizing its cost base and improving margins to further strengthen its foundation for sustainable and profitable growth. With a young average fleet and solid liquidity position, we remain enthusiastic about the name.

Additionally, supplier of residential thermal, comfort and security solutions, Resideo Technologies, Inc. (REZI) outperformed over the period. Despite a challenging macro environment, positive organic sales growth and structural cost efficiencies drove margin expansion. Encouraging commentary around improving consumer demand trends, new product offerings and cross-selling opportunities further aided share price appreciation. Also in the quarter, President and CEO, Jay Geldmacher, announced his intention to retire from his executive and board roles in 2025. Once a successor is identified, Mr. Geldmacher expects to become a senior advisor to the company to facilitate a smooth transition. In our view, this leadership change is indicative of the successful completion of the transformation plan the team initiated following the spin-out from Honeywell. We believe the company is entering a new phase of sustainable growth which will be driven by a secular preference for more connected smart home solutions and product innovation.

In contrast, logistics and cash management services provider, Brink’s Company (BCO) underperformed in the period. Despite the delivery of strong organic revenue growth and margin improvement across both the ATM managed services and digital retail solutions segments, currency headwinds and softness in its global services business drove an earnings miss. Although management lowered full year guidance, BCO continues to win margin accretive business, such as its recently announced long-term partnership with Sainsbury’s, one of the largest supermarkets in the United Kingdom.

Meanwhile, the company is making substantial investments in its technology platforms and returning capital to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases.

Shares of manufacturer and distributor of floorcovering products, Mohawk Industries Inc. (MHK) declined in the period as consumer demand and pricing remain under pressure due to secular headwinds in the housing market. Although quarterly earnings results were in-line with expectations, management lowered near-term guidance to account for losses resulting from recent U.S. hurricanes. Meanwhile, management continues to successfully execute on productivity and cost restructuring initiatives, while also preparing the business for share gains as demand recovers. In our view, MHK’s healthy balance sheet and progress managing through economic cycles should help the company benefit from long-term growth in residential remodeling, new home construction and commercial projects.

Lastly, U.S. direct-to-consumer pool and spa care services company, Leslie’s Inc. (LESL) traded lower following another consecutive quarter of disappointing financial and operating results. Soft consumer demand driven by weather-related headwinds and continued price sensitivity on large discretionary purchases weighed on the top-line. Product margins also remained under pressure as the company struggled to unwind its higher-cost inventory. In response, LESL’s new executive leadership team introduced three key strategic initiatives focused on personalizing communication and product offerings by leveraging local consumer information and data across different markets while improving existing-store sales and productivity. Although we have been deeply disappointed with this investment, we are optimistic the new efforts will drive meaningful improvements to the business and position the company for long-term success. At today’s valuation, LESL appears to have more upside than downside and the company’s loyal client base, vertically integrated supply chain, scale advantage and seamless customer experience remain differentiators.

We did not initiate or exit any positions in the quarter.

We believe the U.S. economy looks healthy and expect corporate profits to prove resilient. Although interest rates will likely settle at a higher structural level compared to the past decade and potential policies around tariffs and immigration present uncertainty, we think market friendly policies such as tax cuts and deregulation should support the broadening of the market beyond the Magnificent Seven. Once the performance gap between mega-cap stocks and smaller company counterparts narrows, our portfolios should be rewarded. We strongly believe the disciplined investor that stays the course and consistently owns differentiated businesses with solid competitive positioning and robust balance sheets will deliver superior returns over time.

Investing in small-cap companies is more risky and volatile than investing in large cap companies. A concentrated portfolio may be subject to greater volatility than a more diversified portfolio. The intrinsic value of the stocks in which the portfolio invests may never be recognized by the broader market. The portfolio is often concentrated in fewer sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market. Investing in equity stocks is risky and subject to the volatility of the markets.

Footnotes

1 The “Magnificent Seven” are the largest stocks in the S&P 500 Index driving market performance: Apple Inc. (AAPL), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Meta Platforms Inc. (META), Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA) and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA).

2 Hobson, Mellody and John W. Rogers Jr. “What the Stock market Taught Us This Year: Don’t Fall for These Investing Traps.” Wall Street Journal, 5 December 2023.

Past performance does not guarantee future results. For the one-year period ended 12/31/24 and since inception on April 30, 2020, the performance (net of fees) for the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite was +11.46% and +14.88%, respectively. For the one-year period ended 12/31/24 and since inception on April 30, 2020 of the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite, the performance for the Russell 2000 Value Index was +8.05% and +15.59%, respectively. And over the same periods the Russell 2000 Index delivered +11.54% and +13.56%, respectively. Ariel Composite Net of Fees returns are calculated by deducting the actual monthly advisory fee (on an asset-weighted basis) applicable to all accounts in the composite, using the fee rates in place as of the most recent calendar quarter-end. Gross returns do not reflect the deduction of advisory fees. Client returns will be reduced by advisory fees and such other expenses as may be incurred in the management of the account. Advisory fees are described in Part 2 of Ariel’s Form ADV. Returns assume the reinvestment of dividends and other earnings. Returns are expressed in U.S. dollars. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. The Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite differs from its primary benchmark in that it has dramatically fewer holdings and more concentration in fewer sectors.

The opinions expressed are current as of the date of this commentary but are subject to change. The information provided in this commentary does not provide information reasonably sufficient upon which to base an investment decision and should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no guarantee that any of the views expressed will come to fruition or any investment will perform as described.

As of 12/31/24, OneSpaWorld Holdings, Ltd. constituted 11.7% of the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite (representative portfolio); Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings, Ltd. 8.4%; Resideo Technologies, Inc. 5.4%; Brink’s Company 8.5%; Mohawk Industries, Inc. 3.8%; and Leslie’s, Inc. 1.7%. Portfolio holdings are subject to change. The performance of any single portfolio holding is no indication of the performance of other portfolio holdings of the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite.

A glossary of financial terms provided herein may be found on our website at www.arielinvestments.com.

Index returns reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings. Indexes are unmanaged, and investors cannot invest directly in an index. The Russell 2000® Value Index measures the performance of the small-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 2000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios, lower forecast growth and lower sales per share historical growth. Its inception date is June 1, 1993. The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of the small-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. The Russell 2000 Index is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index representing approximately 10% of the total market capitalization of that index. It includes approximately 2,000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. Its inception date is January 1, 1984. Russell® is a trademark of London Stock Exchange Group, which is the source and owner of the Russell Indexes' trademarks, service marks and copyrights. Neither Russell nor its licensors accept any liability for any errors or omissions in the Russell Indexes or underlying data and no party may rely on any Russell Indexes and/or underlying data contained in this communication. No further distribution of Russell data is permitted without Russell's express written consent. Russell does not promote, sponsor or endorse the content of this communication. The S&P 500® Index is widely regarded as the best gauge of large-cap U.S. equities. It includes 500 leading companies and covers approximately 80% of available U.S. market capitalization. Its inception date is March 4, 1957.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: JSML: Cautious Outlook But A Clear Improvement Over Russell 2000 Growth ETFs
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Janus Henderson Small Cap Growth Alpha ETF (NASDAQ:JSML) has outperformed its benchmark, represented by the iShares Russell 2000 Growth ETF (IWO) since its inception on February 24, 2016. However, current selections show decelerating earnings growth, leading me to believe the fund's proprietary strategy overemphasizes historical results and growth rates instead of expected ones. While I appreciate the fund's dedication to selecting low-risk, high-quality stocks, I'm slightly skeptical of its near-term prospects, so I'm only assigning it a "hold" rating today. I look forward to explaining why and comparing JSML with some alternatives in the article below.

JSML Overview

Strategy Discussion

The Janus Henderson website lists three reasons to invest in JSML, which I've paraphrased below.

1. The primary objective is to identify small-cap companies with the potential for long-term sustainable growth.

2. A proprietary system evaluates small-cap stocks on ten fundamental factors, including sales growth rates, operating profit, earnings per share, and return on invested capital.

3. The system seeks high risk-adjusted returns by avoiding small-cap stocks without solid fundamentals.

Growth, quality, and risk management are at the heart of the proprietary system, but it's crucial to know strong growth is not consistently emphasized. To illustrate, consider how I have ranked JSML on key fundamental factors since my rankings began in July 2024. The sample size is 16.

Notice how JSML consistently ranks well on risk and quality, but its #13/16 average growth rank suggests the factor actually isn't very important in the process. That's probably because the strategy eliminates many ultra-high-growth stocks that tend to be more speculative than stable, contributing to the fund's average #2/16 risk rank.

As for the other factors, JSML's average #5/16 value rank is solid, though its momentum and sentiment ranks are typically poor. The latter indicates its selections are priced cheaply for a good reason (i.e., poor earnings revisions from Wall Street analysts), so I'm inclined to believe the system doesn't track statistics like these that can be important catalysts for growth.

Performance Analysis

Since its inception, the Small Cap Alpha Index has gained an annualized 13.07% compared to 11.42% for the Russell 2000 Growth Index. The fund's 0.30% accounts for most of the fund's tracking error, ultimately reducing returns to 12.74% per year. IWO's expense ratio is 0.24%, so the impact is a little less, but you can also go with the Vanguard Russell 2000 Growth ETF (VTWG), whose expense ratio is just 0.10%.

The next chart compares JSML's total returns since its inception with the Vanguard Small Cap Growth ETF (VBK), the SPDR S&P 600 Small Cap Growth ETF (SLYG), and the iShares Russell 2000 Growth ETF (IWO). As shown, JSML's 188.58% total return was noticeably better than SLYG and IWO and marginally better than VBK.

Still, these returns are impressive, given how small-cap growth stocks have lagged behind their larger counterparts since JSML's inception and that VBK actually holds several mid-cap stocks. To illustrate, VBK's weighted average market cap is about $10.6 billion, compared to $3.4 billion for JSML. VBK's strong returns are likely due to its wider selection universe, and not necessarily a result of a superior strategy.

An analysis on Portfolio Visualizer adds some additional color to JSML's historical risk and return features. Surprisingly, it was the most volatile of the four, evidenced by a 23.18% annualized standard deviation figure. Its 36.16% drawdown from September 2021 to September 2022 was also the highest, but overall, JSML had solid downside risk-adjusted returns, evidenced by a 0.87 Sortino Ratio. This ratio is more useful than the Sharpe Ratio because only downside risk matters to investors. Logically, investors have no issue with volatility resulting in abnormally high returns.

Recall that high risk-adjusted returns were one of the reasons to invest in JSML, so it seems to have accomplished that. However, let's dig deeper into the fund's composition and fundamentals to learn more.

JSML Analysis

Sector Allocations: Overweight Industrials / Health Care

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between JSML, VBK, SLYG, and IWO. I also included the Pacer US Small Cap Cash Cows Growth Leaders (CAFG) because, despite a relatively poor track record against JSML, it currently offers compelling fundamentals and could make a good satellite holding. JSML and CAFG overlap by just 14.43%.

As shown, JSML overweights Industrials by 9.21% on average, with offsets in Consumer Discretionary, Energy, Real Estate, and Technology. Between Industrials and Health Care, JSML allocates over 50%, so it's a pretty concentrated fund despite holding 202 stocks.

Backtesting JSML: 150 Stocks / 98% By Weight

Before we evaluate JSML's fundamentals, I want to present the results of a three-year backtest of JSML's current holdings and weights, with rebalancings set to quarterly. Using Portfolio Visualizer's backtest tool, I was able to do this for 150/202 stocks, which comprise 98% of the fund. Here are the results.

These results are so good that I have to assume recent performance is a key part of JSML's selection process. This group of stocks actually gained 2.20% in 2022 compared to a 19.64% decline for the ETF and then bounced back with a tremendous 46.78% gain the following year. I haven't come across any ETF that can claim top-level performance in both years, so to say I'm skeptical would be an understatement.

Fundamentals By Top 25 Holdings

With this in mind, please consider the following table that highlights selected fundamental metrics for JSML's top 25 holdings, which comprise 50.81% of the portfolio. Please note this concentration level is substantially higher than VBK, SLYG, and IWO and slightly more than CAFG despite having twice the number of constituents.

Here are three additional observations to consider:

1. Recall how JSML's two standout features are risk management and high quality, which this table nicely demonstrates. First, JSML's 1.11 five-year beta is substantially lower than the broad-based growth funds (VBK, SLYG, and IWO) and supported by high allocations to Health Care stocks like Corcept Therapeutics (CORT) and Catalyst Pharmaceuticals (CPRX). Second, JSML's return on assets, return on equity, and return on total capital figures are excellent, reflecting the manager's preference for capital efficiency. I noticed how only 8/202 holdings (2.67% by weight) have a negative ROTC compared to 396/1060 (28.99% by weight) for IWO, so there's clearly a substantial improvement in quality that, I believe, is responsible for JSML's outperformance.

2. On the other hand, JSML's forward-looking growth statistics aren't as strong as its peers. For example, its 11.85% one-year estimated earnings per share growth rate is 2-6% worse than its peers, but interestingly, its 17.90% three-year historical earnings growth rate is the best of the bunch. This begs the question of which statistic is more relevant, and in my view, it's the former. Small-cap growth stocks, by definition, are immature, and you want to see growth rates accelerate, not decelerate.

DoubleVerify Holdings (DV) is a nice example of the disconnect. Its three-year historical earnings growth rate is 93.29%, but its one-year estimated growth rate is only 23.04%, and investors have reacted negatively with a 45.90% price decline over the last year. I also found that stocks with decelerating earnings growth gained 10.96% over the last year on average compared to 35.74% for those with accelerating earnings growth, so I think estimated rates definitely matter, but it's not something JSML's managers seem to emphasize.

3. The offset to weaker growth rates are more attractive valuation ratios. JSML trades at just 15.79x forward earnings, and if you view it from a growth-at-a-reasonable-price perspective, it's a decent play. When dividing each ETF's forward P/E ratios by their one-year estimated earnings per share growth rates, we get the following:

  • JSML: 15.79x / 11.85% = 1.33x
  • VBK: 25.81x / 16.28% = 1.59x
  • SLYG: 18.45x / 15.12% = 1.22x
  • IWO: 20.59x / 18.26% = 1.13x
  • CAFG: 16.59x / 14.00% = 1.19x

These modified PEG ratios indicate investors should avoid VBK, as it's just too pricey for the growth offered. IWO is attractive but too speculative based on its poor quality metrics, while SLYG offers a nice broad-based compromise. As for JSML and CAFG, they are both high-quality funds, but CAFG looks to have a slight edge at the moment. The challenge is the ETF is too new to know whether these fundamental advantages will continue.

Investment Recommendation

After reviewing JSML's historical results, factor rankings, and fundamentals, I'm stuck between a "buy" and a "hold" rating. I like its consistency in emphasizing low-risk, high-quality stocks trading at attractive valuations. However, I'm discouraged by its constituents' decelerating earnings growth rates, which is an anomaly in the small-cap growth space. I also get the impression that JSML's proprietary strategy overemphasizes recent returns that might not be relevant for the segment. I will err on the side of caution today with a "hold" rating, but for current IWO shareholders, I strongly recommend switching. JSML filters out most of the junk stocks in the Russell 2000 Index, and in the long run, that strategy should be successful.

data.title: Equinor: External Factors And Internal Decisions Can Push Stock Price To A New All-Time High
data.description:
Investment thesis: Equinor (NYSE:EQNR) already looked like a good investment bet for this year because of rising natural gas prices in Europe, which seems to be a trend with staying power. The announcement that it intends to cut investments into green energy in half further boosts its profitability prospects since its green sector has been losing money. It may help cement a higher dividend payment, help to cut debt, and free up money to pursue more profitable investments such as upstream oil & gas production. The improvements in Equinor's profitability have the potential to push its stock price to a new all-time high as soon as this year.

European natural gas storage situation worsens, Equinor responds by focusing investment on upstream production output at expense of loss-making renewables sector.

I last covered Equinor at the start of the year. At that point, European natural gas inventories were trending just below the five-year average, after spending most of last year in glut territory. At the time, the market was just starting to notice that there might be a problem. Since then, inventories continued to decline, and are now about 18% below the five-year average and about 1/3 lower compared with this time last year.

The emerging European energy crisis is not widely acknowledged by the market or the media just yet, but it is getting harder to ignore. European natural gas prices are likely to move higher and remain elevated for the year and perhaps beyond, which bodes well for Equinor as Europe's largest natural gas supplier. At the start of the year, when I wrote my previous article on Equinor, it was still not entirely clear that Europe is facing a major energy crisis, but with the trend of declining inventories continuing, it is becoming increasingly clear. Equinor's earnings may be further boosted as it recently decided to cut its green energy investments. It has been a loss-making segment of the business, so at least it will not weigh on its earnings as much as it did in the past.

Equinor's latest results, and how changes to its investment strategy, are likely to help it improve on future results.

  • Equinor's poor 2024 financial results are hard to shake as an integral part of the market's perception of its future outlook.

Equinor's results for 2024 were less than impressive, with a decline in net income of 26% compared with the prior year, to $8.83 billion. For the fourth quarter, net income came in at just under $2 billion, which was down 23% compared with the same quarter from the previous year. It was a slight improvement compared with the whole year's result, and it may be a reflection of natural gas prices starting to rise in Europe in the fourth quarter, as the supply/demand situation was starting to tighten, which bodes well for this year's outlook, given significantly higher natural gas prices in that market. The poor results can in part be attributed to the fact that European natural gas prices were lower than they were in 2023 for most of 2024. Oil prices were also weak in 2024, on constant forecasts of an impending glut, even as there was a significant shortfall in the global supply/demand equation for the year.

While markets tend to be forward-looking, the poor results that we see in the rearview mirror contributed to the lackluster performance of Equinor's stock price in 2024. As I shall explain, the forward-looking view is somewhat clouded by increasingly unreliable institutional signals, as well as some degree of public complacency.

As we can see, the renewable energy segment of the company is steadily losing money. For this reason, the news that Equinor is cutting renewable energy investments by 50% to $5 billion for the next two years while diverting more money to achieve higher oil & gas production can be translated to mean that Equinor is set to earn higher returns on investment in coming years.

As I shall explain, the outlook for the European natural gas market is looking increasingly favorable for companies that can sell natural gas into that market. The trend is structural, not just temporary. Similarly, the outlook for oil prices is currently depressed by institutional forecasters, which as I have been pointing out for some months seem overly concerned with their agenda, rather than providing markets with beneficial guidance.

Equinor is targeting 10% growth in its upstream oil & gas output, which complements current oil & gas market trends that are also favorable for Equinor's financial performance prospects going forward. Add to it the prospects of a reduced drag on its earnings due to the growing renewable energy sector, thanks to its decision to drastically cut spending on it, and Equinor can arguably be considered to be one of the best prospective investment choices out there in the energy industry.

Europe's tightening natural gas market remains the main positive factor in evaluating Equinor's prospects.

Equinor is now the EU's largest natural gas supplier after the EU & Russia started their process of economic divorce in 2022. This puts it in a position of dependence to some degree on the price movements of the EU natural gas market.

Just to get a sense of Equinor's price advantage over LNG competitors, as well as the potential to profit from higher natural gas prices in Europe, as we can see, the price difference between Equinor's cost of providing Europe with gas mostly through pipelines versus LNG spot prices is substantial. I should mention that the TTF spot price is currently over $17 and most likely it is going higher this year. What this means for Equinor is that it can comfortably raise contract gas prices, without any risk of losing market share to LNG exporters.

The reason why the European natural gas market is increasingly moving in a favorable direction for Equinor's prospects is because of a trend that went largely unnoticed. Since the spring of last year, a steady decline in Europe's natural gas inventories from historically high levels relative to the five-year average brought inventory levels down to five-year average levels by the end of last year. The markets mostly interpreted it as a normal decline triggered by an overabundance of inventories all the way to the last few months of 2024. Since then, inventories continued to decline, and they are now about 18% below the five-year average.

As I pointed out in a recent article on Shell (SHEL), which I also see as being well-positioned to benefit from Europe's emerging energy crisis, since the start of the year, US LNG exports that went to Europe rose from about 2/3 to almost 9/10 of all shipments. Despite the surge in US LNG shipments to Europe, storage levels continued to fall below the five-year average. Incidentally, current inventory levels are now 35% below levels seen at the same time last year.

A word on the possible return of Russian gas to Europe within the context of the Trump administration accelerating its peace efforts.

Equinor recently stated that Europe may need to contract for as many as 350 extra shipments of LNG this year to prepare for next winter. We are gradually seeing a reaction to the trend that I have been highlighting and trading on since the fall. It is a delayed reaction, which, I believe, has been a delayed conversation because up until recently it was an inconvenient topic to discuss from a geopolitical point of view. Among other things, admitting that there is a supply issue is equivalent to admitting that Europe's energy security is at best precarious without significantly higher Russian gas imports, which is a very sensitive political subject for Europeans.

The arrival of the second Trump presidency is changing the dynamics of the situation, even as Europe's natural gas supply/demand dynamics also continue to gradually worsen. Recently, we learned that there was a debate among EU member states on the future role of Russian natural gas supplies in the union. The debate revealed very deep rifts. There are also practical impediments, such as Russia's frozen assets that the EU used to secure a $35 billion loan for Ukraine by borrowing money that is set to be paid back with proceeds taken from the frozen assets. It will take many years to pay the loan back with those proceeds. I'd imagine that Russia will want to get that money back before any economic relations will be restored. Finally, it is not entirely certain that Russia will want to once more become reliant on the European market for its gas. Asia is a larger, growing market, with fewer geopolitical complications, from Russia's perspective.

Investment implications:

  • Equinor is set to benefit from internal as well as external factors.

Even if peace breaks out between Russia and Ukraine this year, it will not be business as usual between Russia & the EU. The EU lacks the political cohesion to restore relations, even if some member states want to see it happen because many nations in the EU are passionately opposed. There is one pipeline left out of the four connecting Germany & Russia, which at some point may still be brought back into operation. Germany will face pressure from other EU members to avoid restoring energy and other economic relations, so it remains to be seen how it will play out. Russia may not be overly enthusiastic about resuming large-scale energy exports to Europe, either. It still hopes to strike a deal with China on the Power of Siberia 2 project, which is set to re-route 50 Bcm of West Siberia gas from the Western markets to the East.

Equinor's clear effort to shore up its profitability by cutting back on renewables investments, while continuing to expand its upstream oil & gas output, bodes well for its future financial outlook. Combined with the favorable external situation, which increasingly suggests natural gas prices in Europe will remain elevated for the foreseeable future, despite recent geopolitical developments that may lead some to believe that we are set to see a reset to the pre-2022 situation in Europe.

Just to put Equinor's potential upside into context, in 2022, when European natural gas prices reached all-time highs, Equinor's net earnings almost tripled compared with 2021, to $28.7 billion. I do not expect natural gas prices to spike to levels we saw in 2022 this year or perhaps any year going forward. However, it increasingly looks like prices are going higher and the increase will be more sustained. Adding Equinor's potential increase in profitability from its reduction in renewables and I see a clear potential for Equinor's stock price to surpass the all-time high reached in 2022, in other words, it could roughly double from here.

  • Risks to my thesis.

The last time I covered Equinor there was only one major risk factor. The weather can influence natural gas demand in Europe, perhaps to a greater degree than anywhere else. It is not only the temperature that dictates natural gas demand but also wind & sunshine since the European grid is now dominated by these sources of electricity, more so than any other source. As of now, we have the added uncertainty of what the European energy market will look like once a now-expected peace process reshapes EU-Russian relations. As of right now, I do not see a path of complete rapprochement and a return to pre-2022 realities, but things can move fast, and anything is possible, even the improbable.

  • Fundamentals in the driving seat when it comes to the European gas market, and Equinor is well-positioned to take advantage.

Germany recently asked for a reprieve from Europe's rule that demands gas storage sites to be filled to 90% by November 1, each year. The decision is credited with natural gas prices declining. This does not change the fact that for almost a year now, there has been a significant supply/demand imbalance and the gap between current storage levels and the five-year average keeps getting wider. At some point, probably soon, Europeans will have to bid up the price of natural gas to fill those storage facilities to adequate levels, even if they will not get to 90%. The very fact that storage levels will not be at those comforting levels will drive natural gas prices higher, and Equinor's financial results are likely to see a significant improvement on those higher prices going forward. Its stock price will benefit accordingly, along with potentially higher dividend payments going forward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Existing Home Sales Retreat 4.9% In January
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Existing home sales began the year with their first decline in four months. According to the National Association of Realtors (NAR), sales retreated 4.9% from December to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.08 million units in January, the largest monthly drop since November 2022. The latest reading came in below the expected 4.13 million.

Despite the monthly decline, existing home sales are 2.0% higher than a year ago, marking the fourth consecutive month of year-over-year gains following 37 months of declines.

"Mortgage rates have refused to budge for several months despite multiple rounds of short-term interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve," said NAR Chief Economist Lawrence Yun. "When combined with elevated home prices, housing affordability remains a major challenge."

"More housing supply allows strongly qualified buyers to enter the market," Yun added. "But for many consumers, both increased inventory and lower mortgage rates are necessary for them to purchase a different home or become first-time homeowners."

Background on Existing Home Sales

Existing home sales measures the monthly sales of previously owned single-family homes. It makes up a huge part of the residential real estate market, as 90% of purchased homes are previously-owned according to the National Association of Realtors. More importantly, there exists a strong correlation between purchases of existing homes and consumer spending. An increase in existing home sales can indirectly stimulate economic activity with increased consumer spending on new furnishings and appliances. Alternatively, a sustained drop in existing home sales often foreshadows a downturn in the economy.

Here is a snapshot of the data series, which comes from the National Association of Realtors. The data since January 1999 was previously available in the St. Louis Fed's FRED repository and is now only available for the last twelve months.

Over this time frame, we clearly see the real estate bubble, which peaked in 2005 and then fell dramatically. Sales were volatile for the first year or so following the Great Recession, with monthly sales as low as 3.45 million units to as high as 5.44 million units. We have seen that same volatility following the most recent recession, with sales ranging between 3.85 million units to 6.73 million units.

Existing Home Sales: The Population-Adjusted Reality

Now let's examine the data with a simple population adjustment. The Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates show a 22.9% increase in the US population since the turn of the century. The snapshot below is an overlay of the NAR's annualized estimates with a population-adjusted version.

Existing home sales are 22.0% below the NAR's January 2000 estimate. The population-adjusted version is 35.8% below the turn-of-the-century sales.

Existing Home Sales: Median Price

The median price for existing homes fell for a third straight month in January, down 1.7% to $396,900, the lowest level since May (non-seasonally adjusted). Compared to one year ago, the median price for an existing home has increased 4.8%, marking the 19th consecutive month of year-over-year increases.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include: iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nu Holdings: I Am Buying The Dip After Q4 Results
data.description:
It has been almost a year since I made my Seeking Alpha debut, covering the stock of Nu Holdings (NYSE:NU). On Thursday, the company reported its Q4 and 2024 earnings results that fell short of investors' expectations, making the stock open with a negative gap of -10.7%, the largest since going public in late 2021.

Since its all-time high of $15.59, the shares have had a considerable drawdown, bringing the price to $10.82, and in this analysis, I will explain why I believe this is a great opportunity to "buy the dip" and be long on Nu's stock.

Q4 Earnings: Concerning in USD, But Not as Much in BRL

The main obstacle of Nu in the quarter was definitely the forex effect, which I had previously outlined as a key risk factor. In Q4, the Brazilian real depreciated -13% against the US dollar, and the financials suffered significantly.

To provide a sense of the FX magnitude, Nu grew its purchase volume to $32.2 billion, representing a -1% decline from a year ago. However, the purchase volume went up on a completely different trajectory of 20% on a forex-neutral basis. Simultaneously, the total portfolio grew to $20.7 billion at 14% vs. 45% on a forex-neutral basis.

An investor who analyzes these growth rates exclusively in US dollars might have a bearish outlook on the company's growth compared to previous quarters. Nonetheless, as displayed above, the neo bank maintains strong growth rates in revenue and EPS despite dropping in earlier quarters in its primary currency. However, the deposits and loan portfolio remain at growth rates similar to those of previous quarters in BRL terms.

User Growth, NIM, and Delinquencies

Nu is well known for its large customer base, and in Q4, it continued to attract 4.5 million new customers, reaching a total of 114.2 million. This made it the third-largest financial institution in terms of the number of customers in Brazil, and in Mexico, it exceeded the 10 million customer target. However, a negative aspect of the quarter that wasn't an FX difference was a drop of -50 bps in the activity rates, but these remained elevated at 83.1%.

At the same time, the net interest margin contracted by -210 bps compared to the past six months, mainly explained by three factors.

  1. Less risky customer profiles in Brazil, lowering yields
  2. Increased funding costs in Mexico and Colombia, following deposit strategy
  3. FX movements

In addition, although net interest income continued to grow at a stunning level of 57% YoY FXN, it has reduced considerably compared to the YoY growth of the past three quarters of 63%, 77%, and 93%.

Considering all these factors and a slight rise in expenses, Nu's efficiency ratio ended at an enviable 29.9%, which is uncommon for a bank and once again demonstrates the operational leverage of running a fully digital business.

Last, 15-90-day non-performing loans started to have a positive trajectory for the third consecutive quarter, which allowed 90+ NPL to finally have a declining figure since Q1 2021, sitting at 7.0%.

Nu's Valuation, Cheaper Than Ever

Previously, Nu's valuation was challenging to assess as its PE ratios were too high. Nonetheless, as the company has become more profitable, obtaining a fascinating net income margin of 32.60%, the PE ratio now looks reasonable. Currently, the forward PE of Nu stands at 20.4x based on a share price of $10.82 and an expected EPS of $0.53. With the drop in share price, this is definitely an undervalued figure, providing a PEG ratio of 0.90x based on an expected EPS growth rate of 29.3% and a trailing PE ratio of 26.4x, providing an opportunity to buy the dip.

Risks to a Bullish Thesis

The obvious risk is a continued depreciation of the Brazilian currency against the US dollar, making the financial statement translation less favorable. In Brazil, there have been consistent fiscal policy concerns about the fiscal deficit, and the president is not particularly in the ideological spectrum of presidents such as Javier Milei or Donald Trump with DOGE.

In addition, it is unlikely that Nu will remain with these high growth rates forever, and at some point, these would have to normalize as has been occurring. Yet, the opportunity to monetize 114.2 million consumers is huge and will remain for a prolonged time. In addition, the company is preparing for an international expansion outside Latin America and plans to enter a new market by the end of this year and consider moving its headquarters to the United Kingdom.

I am Buying the Dip

Although the quarter wasn't necessarily favorable due to lower growth rates in revenue, earnings, and NIM, these metrics remain strong. In addition, a more favorable non-performing loan environment and a lower efficiency ratio that once again demonstrated the business's operational leverage. Finally, with a valuation that is cheaper than ever at 20.4x forward earnings, I will take the opportunity to double my position on Nu, taking a discount of more than 30% from all-time highs, and continue to rate the stock as a "strong buy."

data.title: Allspring Index Asset Allocation Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Global central banks continued cutting rates in the fourth quarter, with the Federal Reserve (Fed) determined to further support economic growth. The European Central Bank continued to cut at every meeting while the Bank of England was more careful as inflation there remained sticky. The Chinese central bank and the government provided more stimulus that gave short-term support to emerging markets. However, while Japan is keen to normalize its interest rate policy further, it held off with rate hikes in the fourth quarter as the yen started to strengthen.

At around 2.4%, the level of headline inflation in the U.S. is still above target, but the overall trend is pointing downward at a slower pace than in the previous quarters. Near quarter-end, we saw some inflation levels stabilize at slightly higher levels as the U.S. economy seems to have reaccelerated on the hope of tax and rate cuts. With respect to growth, the big news in the fourth quarter was the clean sweep for the Republicans in the U.S. election. Initially, we saw a strong rally in equities and bond yields rose in anticipation of strong fiscal policy support. Outside of the U.S., the growth picture remained bleak with the eurozone and the United Kingdom barely growing and China fighting deflation.

Over the quarter, the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) was up 2.41% and the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index was down 3.13%, as rates on the long end of the curve went up despite rate cuts by the Fed.

Current performance maybe lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.80%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.75%. The manager has contractually committed, through January 31, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.75% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any) from funds in which the underlying affiliated master portfolios and funds invest and from money market funds, and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. All other acquired fund fees and expenses from the affiliated master portfolios and funds are included in the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap may be increased or the commitment to maintain the cap may be terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Overall Asset Allocation

The fund's stock holdings seek to replicate the holdings of the S&P 500 Index, while its bond holdings seek to replicate the holdings of the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index.

The fund's neutral target allocation is 60% stocks and 40% bonds. When incorporating the impact from TAA positions, at quarter-end, the fund had an effective target allocation of 67.9% stocks, 46.4% bonds, with effective cash at -14.3%. The effective target allocation reflects the fund's quarter-end exposure from long U.S.10-year Treasury (US10Y) and long S&P 500 Index trades implemented using futures contracts. While the S&P 500 Index advanced this quarter, only four sectors (consumer discretionary, information technology, financials, and communication services) produced positive returns.

Tactical Asset Allocation (TAA) Overlay

TAA detracted approximately 21 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) from performance during the quarter. With respect to TAA activity, we started the quarter with a long S&P 500 7.75% position in place; the position was reduced in mid-November after the post-election rally and then increased back again near the end of the month. We also started the quarter with a 10/30 steepener that we closed on November 27 and converted it to an outright long 10-Year U.S. Treasury 6.95% position.

PORTFOLIO ALLOCATIONS (%)

Sources: Bloomberg Finance L.P., Standard & Poor's, and Allspring Global Investments.

Allocations are subject to change and may have changed since the date specified. Allocations shown are as of the end of the period. Small differences in rounding may occur and percentages may not add up to 100%.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

Monetary policy in the U.S. is continuing to get looser, though the Fed likely will take a more cautious approach given the still-elevated core inflation readings and the prospect of more Trump-induced fiscal stimulus to propel U.S. economic growth higher.

Real gross domestic product growth in the U.S. is likely to rise from 2.5% to around 3%, though medium-term growth is likely to moderate back to trend growth of around 2.5%, as fiscal stimulus is already very loose and the marginal impact might not be as big as under the first Trump administration. The eurozone is dragged down by France and Germany, with Germany probably already in recession and consumer sentiment, as well as manufacturing, very weak. Equity earnings momentum has continued to slow into the fourth quarter, though it is likely to pick back up to 12% later in the year, driven by lower interest rates and continued fiscal stimulus as well as robust U.S. consumer sentiment. International earnings are likely to remain volatile and majorly affected by potential U.S. trade tariffs. On the other hand, more fiscal stimulus from China should support international investor sentiment. Bonds should continue to benefit from further rate cuts, although, shorter term, a lot of optimism is currently priced into the market. The short-term interest rate market is expecting another two cuts in 2025, though if real yields remain elevated, we might see more rate cuts. European central banks are expected to cut much more aggressively this year, and China needs to provide more stimulus to turn consumer sentiment around. Cross-asset wise, we remain overweight equities and bonds versus commodities as inflation continues to slow. Equity wise, we prefer U.S. equities absolute and relative to international markets given the positive growth differential. On the bond side, we prefer international bonds, as weaker growth and inflation may lead to more rate cuts than in the U.S. Inflation-sensitive assets should continue to underperform the market despite Chinese stimulus as the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries Plus is likely to increase production and global commodity demand remains weak.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - U.S. PCE, Japanese Inflation And Tariffs In Focus
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in Review: Tariff Chatter Continues to Drive Market Moves

Markets had another interesting week as US President Donald Trump pledged more tariffs ahead. A potential peace deal appears to be gaining traction between Russia and Ukraine, but this did little to dent the appeal of Safe havens as Gold continues to hold the high ground.

US Equity markets did take a slight hit toward the backend of the week, largely driven by news from Walmart.

The $780 billion retailer reported strong holiday and January sales, thanks to wealthier customers looking for deals. However, it warned that inflation could hurt shoppers, causing its stock value to drop by 6%. This shows that the economic optimism seen after Donald Trump's election may be starting to fade.

Walmart (WMT) acknowledged "uncertain times," which led to a cautious outlook and a $50 billion drop in its market value. Being a key indicator of U.S. spending habits, Walmart's concerns matter. Even Donald Trump admitted that "inflation is back," and research shows consumer confidence in business and jobs fell sharply in January.

Wall Street is also uneasy. David Kelly, a strategist at JPMorgan, warned about risks to economic growth from new policies, including tariffs and deportations. If Walmart is feeling nervous, it could signal a growing wave of fear in the economy.

This was compounded by weak US data, as the S&P Global data showed that U.S. business activity barely grew in February as worries about import tariffs and major government spending cuts increased.

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 both printed fresh highs before a selloff on Thursday and Friday, reflecting the market's concerns about these developments.

On the commodities front, Gold continued its advance this week but did have a bit of a seesaw run. The one concern that bulls may have is that this week's rally failed to print fresh all-time highs. Is this a sign that bullish pressure may be waning?

Oil prices had an interesting week, with gains every day before a significant selloff on Friday left Brent Crude trading flat for the week. Supply jitters continue to persist, but President Trump's wish of lower energy and oil prices clearly is weighing on the minds of market participants. News filtered through today that the US is putting pressure on Iraq to restart the pipeline to Turkey, which could explain the fall in Oil prices, or at least partially so.

On the FX front, the US Dollar struggled this week, largely due to a massive selloff on Thursday. This was a surprise, given that Wednesday FOMC minutes showed the Fed is concerned about the impact of tariffs on inflation. This has led to the idea that rate cuts may be pushed back later in the year than had been previously anticipated.

The Week Ahead: US CPE Will be Key After Hot US CPI data, German Elections are in Focus as Well

Asia Pacific Markets

The main focus this week in the Asia Pacific region for me will be inflation data from Japan, while we also have some Chinese data to monitor.

The week's key focus is Tokyo's inflation data, expected to hold at 3.3% in February. Rising fresh food prices may drive costs up, but energy subsidies should balance this out. The BoJ will monitor if higher food costs, like rice, are affecting consumers. Economic data suggests a slow recovery, with industrial output improving due to exports and retail sales boosted by better wages and more tourists.

China's schedule for economic data is light in the last week of February. On Tuesday, the People's Bank of China is expected to decide on the medium-term lending facility (MLF) rate. Since the focus has shifted to the 7-day reverse repo rate as the main policy tool, no changes to the MLF rate are expected this month. Any surprise, however, could have a knock on effect on emerging markets.

Europe + UK + US

In developed markets, the US CPI release last week really stoked concerns about higher rates for longer. However, Fed Chair Powell was quick to stress how the Fed prefers the PCE data as their inflation gauge and cautioned against reading too much into the CPI release.

Next week's PCE data includes the Fed's preferred inflation measure, the core personal consumer expenditure (PCE) deflator. While the core CPI rose by a concerning 0.4% month-on-month, other data suggests that the core PCE deflator is likely to show a smaller increase of 0.3%. To reach the 2% yearly inflation target, monthly inflation needs to average 0.17%. Due to these factors, it's unlikely we'll see any rate cuts before the September FOMC meeting.

Europe's most industrialized economy faces a big weekend as Germans head to the polls in what will be a key election for Europe as a whole. The German economy faces a host of challenges, while the question of immigration has also been a key campaign point.

The elections could have an impact on both the Euro and German Bunds as well when the market opens on Sunday night.

Chart of the Week

This week's focus is on the US Dollar Index (DXY) after Thursday's selloff and a break of the key level of support at 107.00.

Moving into next week and with the PCE data on the horizon, will the DXY rebound? That is the big question for market participants.

Currently, the DXY has found support at the 100-day MA, resting at 106.47 with a bullish inside bar candle close on Friday.

This leaves me to believe that we could get some bullish strength on Monday and perhaps a retest of the 107.00 handle.

More tariff chatter could help propel the DXY above the support turned resistance at 107.00, however in the absence of tariff chatter the DXY may grind sideways until the release of the PCE Data.

US Dollar Index Daily Chart - February 21, 2025

Key Levels to Consider:

Support

  • 106.47
  • 106.13
  • 105.63

Resistance

  • 107.00
  • 108.00
  • 108.49

Original Post

The Chinese central bank and the government provided more stimulus that gave short-term support to emerging markets. However, while Japan is keen to normalize its interest rate policy further, it held off with rate hikes in the fourth quarter as the yen started to strengthen.

At around 2.4%, the level of headline inflation in the U.S. is still above target, but the overall trend is pointing downward at a slower pace than in the previous quarters. Renewed strength in the U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR, DXY) served as a headwind for non-U.S. stocks, and the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index (Net) gave back -7.60% for U.S. investors.

Portfolio positioning: Overall Asset Allocation

The portfolio’s neutral allocation target is 35% fixed income, 45% U.S. equity, and 20% foreign equity, and at quarter-end, the effective allocation revealed an overweight to both U.S. equity and fixed income investments. With respect to our tactical asset allocation positioning, we started the quarter with a balanced exposure overweight equities and U.S. Treasury bonds. We continued our bias toward U.S. equities over emerging market equities and preference for information technology stocks over the broader index. Over the quarter, we added equity exposure through an overweight to U.S. equities versus Australian and Chinese equities, and we increased our U.S. dollar long position versus the New Zealand dollar, British pound, and euro currencies, as we saw continued strength in the U.S. economy while international markets continued to struggle growth-wise.

Underlying Funds

Six of 10 of the underlying equity investment strategies employed in the fund underperformed their relative style-specific benchmarks over the period. The relative underperformance was broad based, with stock selection being the largest detractor. The fixed income investments did well on a relative basis, with exposure to the securitized and inflation-sensitive securities contributing to relative performance.

PORTFOLIO ALLOCATIONS (%)

Sources: Bloomberg Finance L.P., MSCI, Russell, and Allspring Global Investments.

The effective allocation reflects the effect of the tactical futures overlay that may be in place. Effective allocations are subject to change and may have changed since the date specified. Percent total may not add to 100% due to futures positions.

Portfolio positioning: Outlook

Monetary policy in the U.S. is continuing to get looser, though the Fed likely will take a more cautious approach given the still-elevated core inflation readings and the prospect of more Trump-induced fiscal stimulus to propel U.S. economic growth higher.

Real gross domestic product growth in the U.S. is likely to rise from 2.5% to around 3%, though medium-term growth is likely to moderate back to trend growth of around 2.5%, as fiscal stimulus is already very loose and the marginal impact might not be as big as under the first Trump administration. The eurozone is dragged down by France and Germany, with Germany probably already in recession and consumer sentiment, as well as manufacturing, very weak. Equity earnings momentum has continued to slow into the fourth quarter, though it is likely to pick back up to 12% later in the year, driven by lower interest rates and continued fiscal stimulus as well as robust U.S. consumer sentiment. International earnings are likely to remain volatile and majorly affected by potential U.S. trade tariffs. On the other hand, more fiscal stimulus from China should support international investor sentiment. Bonds should continue to benefit from further rate cuts, although, shorter term, a lot of optimism is currently priced into the market. The short-term interest rate market is expecting another two cuts in 2025, though if real yields remain elevated, we might see more rate cuts. European central banks are expected to cut much more aggressively this year, and China needs to provide more stimulus to turn consumer sentiment around. Cross-asset wise, we remain overweight equities and bonds versus commodities as inflation continues to slow. Equity wise, we prefer U.S. equities absolute and relative to international markets given the positive growth differential. On the bond side, we prefer international bonds, as weaker growth and inflation may lead to more rate cuts than in the U.S. Inflation-sensitive assets should continue to underperform the market despite Chinese stimulus as the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries Plus is likely to increase production and global commodity demand remains weak.
